topics.title: HTTP, REST & Web Fundamentals
topics.description: HTTP (HyperText Transfer Protocol) is a stateless, text-based request-response protocol over TCP. A request contains: method (GET/POST/PUT/DELETE/PATCH), URL, headers, optional body. A response contains: status code (1xx informational, 2xx success, 3xx redirect, 4xx client error, 5xx server error), headers, body. REST (Representational State Transfer) is an architectural style: stateless, client-server, uniform interface (resource URLs + HTTP verbs), cacheable. SOAP is a protocol with XML envelopes, WSDL contracts, and mandatory error handling — more rigid but WS-Security and guaranteed delivery. HTTPS adds TLS (Transport Layer Security) on top of HTTP — encrypts the channel after a certificate handshake.
topics.story: Think of HTTP as the postal system. Every letter (request) has an address (URL), a type stamp (GET/POST — read request or send parcel), and optional contents. Every reply (response) has a status sticker (200 OK, 404 Not Found, 500 Oops). REST says: address each 'thing' (resource) with its own address (/users/42) and use the stamp type to say what to do with it. SOAP is FedEx with a mandatory form, tracking number, and signature receipt — more ceremony but guaranteed delivery. HTTPS is a sealed envelope — the postal worker can see the address (domain) but can't read the letter contents (TLS encrypted body).
topics.analogy: HTTP verbs: GET = reading a book (safe, idempotent, no side effect), POST = submitting a form (creates new resource, not idempotent — each submit creates a new order), PUT = replacing a book on a shelf (idempotent — same result whether done once or 100 times), DELETE = removing the book (idempotent — deleting deleted resource is still 'done'), PATCH = correcting one page (partial update). Idempotent means: calling it N times has the same effect as calling it once. Status codes: 2xx = thumbs up, 3xx = I moved — follow me, 4xx = you messed up, 5xx = I messed up. 401 = 'who are you?' (unauthenticated), 403 = 'I know you, but no' (unauthorized).
topics.memoryAnchor: HTTP methods: GET(read,safe,idempotent), POST(create,not idempotent), PUT(replace,idempotent), PATCH(partial update), DELETE(remove,idempotent), HEAD(metadata only), OPTIONS(CORS preflight). Status codes: 200 OK, 201 Created, 204 No Content, 301 Moved Permanently, 302 Found (temp redirect), 304 Not Modified (cached), 400 Bad Request, 401 Unauthorized (no auth), 403 Forbidden (has auth but no permission), 404 Not Found, 409 Conflict, 422 Unprocessable Entity, 429 Too Many Requests, 500 Internal Server Error, 502 Bad Gateway, 503 Service Unavailable. REST constraints: stateless, client-server, cacheable, uniform interface (resource+verb), layered system, (optional) code-on-demand. Cookie: client-side, max 4KB. Session: server-side, ID in cookie. JWT: self-contained token, signature-verified, stateless.
topics.firstPrinciples: HTTP/1.1 uses persistent connections (keep-alive). HTTP/2 multiplexes multiple requests over one TCP connection (no head-of-line blocking). HTTP/3 runs over QUIC (UDP-based), faster TLS handshake. TLS handshake: client sends supported cipher suites → server replies with certificate → client verifies certificate against CA → symmetric key negotiated → encrypted channel. REST is not a standard — it's Roy Fielding's constraints. An API is RESTful if it satisfies them; most 'REST APIs' are actually just HTTP APIs with JSON. HATEOAS (Hypermedia As The Engine Of Application State) is the final, rarely implemented REST constraint.
topics.bruteForce: All operations as POST to a single endpoint /api (RPC style). Returning 200 for everything including errors — clients must parse the body to know if it succeeded. Storing state in server memory (session) for what should be a stateless API. Using HTTP GET for mutations (adding to cart via a link) — breaks bookmarkability, CDN caching, and browser pre-fetch.
topics.optimizedApproach: Resource-centric URLs (/users/{id}/orders) + correct HTTP verbs. Meaningful status codes — never 200 for errors. Stateless API with JWT (no server-side session). Versioning: /api/v1/ or Accept: application/vnd.app.v2+json header. Pagination: ?page=0&size=20&sort=createdAt,desc. HATEOAS links in responses for discoverability. Rate limiting headers: X-RateLimit-Limit, X-RateLimit-Remaining. ETag + If-None-Match for conditional requests (304 saves bandwidth). CORS: Access-Control-Allow-Origin header, preflight OPTIONS request.
topics.whenToUse: REST: public APIs, mobile backends, microservices, any modern HTTP API. SOAP: enterprise integrations, financial systems with WS-Security, WSDL-defined contracts. GraphQL: complex queries, multiple clients with different data needs (avoids over/under-fetching). gRPC: high-throughput internal microservice communication (binary Protocol Buffers, HTTP/2, streaming). WebSocket: real-time bidirectional (chat, live feeds, collaborative editing).

examples.title: HTTP Methods, Status Codes, and REST Design
examples.explanation: The key interview distinctions: safe vs idempotent (GET is both; DELETE is idempotent but not safe; POST is neither). 401 vs 403 confusion is a classic trap — 401 means authentication missing/invalid, 403 means authentication succeeded but authorization failed. REST API design: always use nouns in URLs (/users, not /getUser), use HTTP verbs for actions, return 201+Location after POST create, return 204 with empty body after DELETE.
examples.realWorldUse: Spring MVC maps these automatically: @GetMapping, @PostMapping, @DeleteMapping. @ResponseStatus(201) sets status code. ResponseEntity<> gives full control. Spring Boot's @RestControllerAdvice centralizes exception-to-status-code mapping. ETag + conditional requests save bandwidth on large JSON responses. Rate limiting with 429 + Retry-After is standard in production APIs (Bucket4j + Redis in Spring).

examples.title: HTTPS, TLS, CORS, and Cookies Deep Dive
examples.explanation: Three separate concepts often confused in interviews: TLS encrypts the channel (not just passwords — everything); CORS is a browser-enforced policy (not server security — a curl call bypasses it); SameSite prevents CSRF for cookie-based auth. The asymmetric/symmetric split in TLS is important: RSA/EC only negotiates the session key, then everything uses fast symmetric AES. Forward secrecy (ECDHE) means even if the server's private key is later stolen, past sessions can't be decrypted.
examples.realWorldUse: Spring Security CSRF protection: enabled by default for browser clients, disabled for stateless APIs (JWT). Spring CORS: configured in SecurityConfig.cors() or @CrossOrigin. Let's Encrypt: free TLS certificates auto-renewed by certbot or Caddy. Nginx terminates TLS before forwarding to Spring Boot (common deployment pattern). HSTS header forces browsers to always use HTTPS for your domain.

problems.title: Design a REST API for an Order Management System
problems.description: Design REST endpoints for an order management system with: Users, Orders (each belongs to a user), and Items (each belongs to an order). Define the URL structure, HTTP method, request body (where applicable), success response code, and error codes for: list all orders for a user, get a specific order, create a new order, update order status, delete a cancelled order, and list items in an order.
problems.hint1: Use nested resources for owned relationships: /users/{userId}/orders.
problems.hint2: PATCH is correct for status-only update (partial). PUT replaces the whole resource.
problems.hint3: DELETE on a non-existent order → 404. DELETE on an order that's already shipped → 409 Conflict.
problems.testCases:
GET /users/42/orders → 200
GET /users/42/orders/7 → 200 or 404
POST /users/42/orders → 201 + Location
PATCH /users/42/orders/7 → 200
DELETE /users/42/orders/7 → 204 or 404 or 409
GET /users/42/orders/7/items → 200

problems.title: HTTP Status Code Classifier
problems.description: Write a method that, given a scenario description string, returns the correct HTTP status code. Handle these scenarios: successful GET, new resource created, DELETE with empty body, resource not found, wrong credentials, correct credentials but wrong role, validation error, duplicate unique field, rate limit hit, upstream service crashed.
problems.hint1: 201 for POST create. 204 for DELETE/PUT with no response body.
problems.hint2: 401 for missing/invalid auth token. 403 for valid token but insufficient permission.
problems.hint3: 409 for duplicate unique constraint. 422 for invalid field values. 429 for rate limiting.
problems.testCases:
GET succeeded → 200
POST created → 201
DELETE nothing to return → 204
Not exist → 404
No token → 401
Not admin → 403
Invalid format → 422
Already registered → 409
Rate limit → 429
DB failed → 500
